column9: Best in Class

column3: All Logitech products must focus on battery access to enable repair and recycling
column6: Removed with damage, but still possible (Example: [MX Master 3s](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Logitech+MX+Master+3S+Battery+Replacement/159781))
column9: 1 Step Removal, No Tools (Example: [Logitech Signature M650](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Logitech+Signature+M650+Battery+Replacement/194957))

column3: A broken cable can mean an early end of life for a product, ensure that the cables can be replaced.
column5: Cables are rigidly attached and soldered on one side preventing replacement (Example: [Zone Wired 2 for Business](https://www.logitech.com/en-us/shop/p/zone-wired-2), [Brio 500](https://www.logitech.com/en-us/shop/p/brio-500-webcam))
column6: Cables are rigidly attached with an internal connector. Replacement is possible but involves damage like removing feet (Example: [G203](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kT8xjLGxhFs))
column7: Cables are rigidly attached with an internal connector. Damage free replacement is possible (Example: [K120](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Logitech+K120+Keyboard+USB+Cable+Replacement/132208))
column8: Cables are removable but a custom / proprietary cable is required (Example: [Zone Learn](https://support.logi.com/hc/en-us/articles/12974063724951-Spare-Part-Zone-Learn), [LogitechG PRO D11 Wheel Base](https://www.logitechg.com/en-us/shop/p/pro-usb-cable))
column9: All cables removable and replaceable with standard connector types (Example: [LogitechG Pro X Headset](https://www.logitechg.com/en-us/shop/p/pro-x-gaming-headset-blue-voice-mic-tech), [G515](https://www.logitechg.com/en-us/shop/p/g515-rapid-tkl))

column3: Consider the most critical priority part, other than the battery, in the assembly. {{Priority Parts}} For more information on priority parts take a look at the [Design for Repair Quick Start Guide](https://docs.google.com/presentation/u/0/d/1J4K0D3cpDy0K1RvMxXSSwTArdLjN7ZuZcCPbjQKPHKQ/view)
column5: Priority parts are permanently integrated or soldered ([Key Switches on MX Mechanical](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Disassembling+Logitech+MX+Mechanical/176320))
column6: Priority parts are accessible with damage, for example entry is hidden by feet or stickers ([Main Switches in G PRO Mouse](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uIwV4WrH6Us))
column7: Priority parts are accessible without damage but buried under unrelated functional layers ([Slip Ring in LogitechG RS50](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1bR2XdrqcLn-PVl8dBIdR6WJhtCWUIPbZk4F1yfXHD_A/edit?usp=sharing))
column8: Priority parts have been surfaced directly behind the primary enclosure and the fasteners are exposed ([Main PCB on Logitech Rally Board 65](https://fccid.io/JNZVR0039/Internal-Photos/Internal-Photos-Part-1-rev-8097353))
column9: Priority parts are directly accessible or accessible via a dedicated easy-access door with no internal disassembly ([Switches on HP ProArt Mouse MD301](https://www.asus.com/us/accessories/mice-and-mouse-pads/proart/proart-mouse-md301/), [Ear Cup Pads on LogitechG Pro X Headset](https://www.ifixit.com/Guide/Logitech+G+Pro+X+Wireless+Ear+Pads+Replacement/170342))

column3: Commonly available tools are defined by EN45554 Table A.3 and include: Phillips, Hex, Torx, Tweezer, Spudger
column5: Requires proprietary tools or hazardous heating/solvent equipment
column6: Requires non-common tools only available to authorized centers
column8: Requires only one commonly available tool
column9: Requires no tools (manual latches, spring-buttons)

column3: Reducing the number of steps decreases the cost, barrier to entry and risk of damage.
column5: Deep, complex teardown required to reach the most common failing component
column6: Deepest priority part requires more than 10 steps to reach
column7: Deepest priority part requires less than 10 steps to reach
column8: Depth is optimized; priority parts reached in 4–6 steps
column9: Ultra-shallow: The priority parts are removed in <3 steps

column3: How well is the product protected from cosmetic damage during disassembly?
column5: Opening requires high heat or heavy prying that guarantees cosmetic damage (warping, scratches) or risks cracking glass/screens just to get inside.
column6: Once the shell is opened, there are exposed ribbon cables or delicate sensors immediately under the seam. A prying tool slipping by 2mm could sever a critical connection.
column7: Design includes "pick-start" notches or specific gaps for tools. Internal components are slightly recessed, but there are still no physical barriers to stop a tool from over-penetrating.
column8: Integrated mechanical stoppers and recessed connectors safeguard sensitive components from tool slips or perimeter impacts.
column9: No tools are required, making accidental internal or cosmetic damage virtually impossible.

column3: Is there user guidance for disassembly?
column5: Internal layout is intentionally opaque to discourage non-expert access
column6: Opening points are hidden under glued rubber feet or regulatory stickers
column7: Opening points, like fasteners, are exposed but there is no guidance
column8: Internal labels identify parts and screws to guide the technician
column9: Internal QR codes or permanent icons guide the disassembly sequence

column3: How easily can the product be reassembled?
column6: Reassembly is "guess-work" (identical cables can be plugged into wrong ports, parts to be installed upside-down or backwards)
column7: Visual cues guide correct re-installation (color-coded connectors or markings)
column8: Error proofing, parts cannot be installed incorrectly (Poke-Yoke, captive screws, magnetic alignment)
column9: Both visual guidance and error proofing

column3: Consider the materials listed in the CMF design spec. Renewable materials include post-consumer and post-industrial recycling. Biobased materials can also be considered if they are widely recycled (PP, PE, PS, HIPS, ABS grades) or seperated for biodegrading.
column6: Renewable material inputs limited. Some colors and materials are still virgin to match the CMF direction
column7: [PCR Pool](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1r5Vgp-g2j7bV0EpCeCGmfo3IU94j7SR6oOviF1J88sM/edit?usp=sharing) is leveraged to maximize the renewable material content of all CMF design parts.
column8: Project qualifies a new material with higher renewable content or unlocks a new color or capability at an existing best in class material (qualifying a new color of 100% PCR ABS)
column9: All CMF design materials are 100% renewable

column3: Think about which materials will wear first. Often this is where a user touches, holds or interacts with the product. This relates both to the material and texture selection. Consider the worst performing colorway, often white.
column5: Materials are likely to age, stain or show wear within 90 days
column6: Materials will not age, stain or show wear in the first 2 years of heavy use
column7: Materials will not age, stain or show wear in the first 3 years of heavy use
column8: Materials will not age, stain or show wear in the first 5 years of heavy use
column9: Materials will never age or will do so in a way that increases the value

column3: Keeping your device clean and fresh is a great way to extend the life and encourage users to keep their devices for longer.
column5: Deep seams and open grilles trap debris, or soft-touch materials degrade when cleaned with isopropyl alcohol.
column6: Flat surfaces are wipeable, but recessed buttons and ports require specialized tools and carry a high risk of liquid damage.
column7: Tight tolerances and flush surfaces eliminate crevices, while chemical-resistant materials support standard household cleaners.
column8: Seals enable full immersion or rinsing for effortless sanitization.
column9: Modular, tool-less "high-touch" parts allow for separate washing or replacement.

column3: Emotional durability is the unspoken emotional experiences between products and consumers. A product with a strong the emotional bond is more likely to be kept for longer, and to see a second life through resale or repair
column5: No customization, modifications or other emotional bonds.
column6: Industry standard software based customizations (key mapping, sensor calibration or RGB lighting)
column7:
Novel software customization (these settings can be shared with others creating an online community)

AND/OR

Hardware cosmetic customization (look and feel)
column8:
Hardware functional customization (meet bespoke user needs, maybe include modularity)

AND/OR

Onboarding involves assembly to create a bond and better understand the construction and value.
column9: The hardware and/or software is open-source to allow user built customizations, mods and repairs.

column3: Materials have to be separated for recycling. This question asks how easily those materials can be separated by typical recycling machines such as shredders and hammers.
column5: More than half of the material weight is glued together or otherwise permanently bonded
column6: Only technically required areas have permanent bonding
column7: Materials not widely recycled, such as fabrics or elastomers, are designed for quick and easy separation before destructive dismantling
column8: No permanent bonding of dissimilar materials but screws are still used for construction resulting in some material loss when shredding
column9: No permanent bonding of dissimilar materials. The assembly is either mono-material or dissimilar materials are designed to separate when shredded (snaps, clips, etc.).

column3: Widely recycled materials are: all metals except magnesium and PP, PE, PS, HIPS, ABS plastic. All other plastic grades, blends and materials are not widely recycled.
column5: None of the CMF design materials are widely recycled
column6: Less than half of the CMF design materials are widely recycled
column7: More than half of the CMF design materials are widely recycled
column8: Only critical to function CMF design materials are not widely recycled (such as a PC screen cover or plenum rated enclosure)
column9: A CMF Design Materials are widely recycled

column3: Labels and coatings can prevent separation or inhibit plastic sorting. IML in the same material as the base part is acceptable. Labels attached with a water soluble adhesive are recyclable. Darker colors inhibit optical sorting. Lighter colors have more value because the color palette can be changed.
column5: Plastic parts are painted and/or covered in labels, IML, PVD or paint
column6: Plastic parts are painted only if there is a functional requirement such as backlit keyboard keys
column7: All plastic parts are free of paint or coatings
column8: All plastic parts are also free of non-recyclable labels
column9: All plastics are in natural, ready to recycle colors. No dark color SKUs in the product plan.

column3: Encouraging the user to properly dispose of their device at the end of life is critical to the circular journey of our products.
column5: No user guidance beyond legal requirement
column6: Recycling and/or take back website linked on packaging
column7: Recycling and/or take back website linked on product
column8: Recycling and/or take back incentives on product
column9: Full product passport showing the origin, value and next life plans for the product's materials
